document:
It started because the lawnmower wouldn’t start. Dead battery again. I pulled it halfway out, cables tangled like old spaghetti, and that’s when the avalanche began—paint cans (dried), a speaker with one torn cone, a broken umbrella, a stack of pizza-stained moving boxes from when we first moved in. Looked like a recycling bin threw up.

Tomas finally said something last week when he couldn’t get to the ski rack. “It’s a museum,” he said. “Of things we don’t do.” I threw a roll of duct tape at his head. Made a dent.

Anyway. Cleared a Saturday. Cold coffee, old trainers, sleeves rolled. Started with the floor—swept up gravel, pine needles, the usual. Found two missing marbles from last summer, a cracked phone charger, and a single skate wheel. Pile system: toss, donate, “maybe later.” The “maybe later” pile grew too fast. Ended up mostly full of hardware with no project attached. Bolts, brackets, lengths of copper pipe. Things I kept thinking, *might need that someday.* Kofi says the definition of “someday” is “never.”

Noor showed up mid-afternoon with two thermoses of soup and didn’t ask questions. Just grabbed a trash bag and started in on the corner near the water heater. We found her dead mouse later. Wrapped it in newspaper. Didn’t make a ceremony.

Donated three crates: mismatched tools, camping chairs with ripped fabric, a bike that might roll if you pushed it downhill first. Hauled two black bags to the curb. Still left with two shelving units that look like they’re holding each other up. 

Left the extension cord coiled on the bench. It’s a start. Maybe this weekend I’ll try to figure out what that one tool is—the one with teeth and a trigger. Looks dangerous. Probably just a hedge trimmer. Probably.

It’s funny how much of your life ends up in a garage. Not the kind of life you post or talk about, but the other kind—the slow accumulation of what you meant to do, or thought you might need, or just forgot. Today I pulled everything out. Not heroically, not with music and momentum, but in slow tugs and pauses, like pulling teeth.

There was a bike from maybe ten years ago, the chain rusted into itself. A box of cables, none of which fit anything I own now. Two plant pots with dirt still in them, dry and cracked like old cake. And tools—honestly, how many screwdrivers does one person need? I sat on an upside-down bucket for a long time just looking at a tarp I’d used once to move a sofa. It still smelled faintly of dust and effort.

The light in there is weak, the kind that makes shadows too thick in the corners. I kept finding things I didn’t remember owning. A pair of work gloves with one finger split open. A stack of magazines about gardening, even though I’ve never gardened. Someone had given them to me once, I think. Ingrid? Maybe. It’s hard to say.

I sorted things into piles: keep, donate, throw. But the lines kept blurring. The suitcase with the broken wheel—I could fix it. The half-used paint tray—might come in handy. The nesting boxes inside boxes, sealed with tape I no longer had the strength to break. 

By late afternoon, everything was back inside, arranged a little better, a little neater. But it didn’t feel finished. More like a pause, a breath held. The car still doesn’t fit. And now I notice how the light hits the floor differently, showing more than it hides. I keep thinking about the tarp, how I ended up wrapping it around some broken shelves instead of tossing it. I don’t know why. Something about not wanting the whole thing to mean nothing.

It’s the same ritual every morning, yet somehow each method feels like a different promise. The French press sits heavy on the counter, its glass belly catching the weak winter light. I grind the beans a little coarse—they should feel like sand between fingers—and let the water bloom just long enough before plunging. It takes patience. Too fast and the coffee bites; too slow and it goes flat. Ingrid always said it tasted like “wet earth after rain,” and though I never understood exactly what she meant, I think I caught a glimpse of it once during a long, quiet weekend at her cabin. Now, every time I lift that plunger, I wait for it—the ghost of something deeper than caffeine.

Then there’s the cone drip, the one Devon showed me after spilling hot water across his kitchen tiles in frustration. Paper filter, swirling pour, the slow concentric circles that steam up my glasses. This one feels deliberate, like a small act of precision in a day that otherwise runs on habit. I’ve burned batches, under-extracted others. The kettle’s whistle startles me sometimes, even after all this time. But when it’s right—the balanced brightness, the way it lingers—I feel, briefly, like I’ve done something properly, even if nothing else follows.

Luca prefers the stovetop pot, the kind that rumbles and hisses like a discontented cat. He says it’s the only way to get “body,” whatever that means. I inherited mine from a neighbor who moved west and left it behind in a cardboard box labeled “misc.” It fits in my palm awkwardly now, the handle loose, the base scored from too many burns. But when it starts to gurgle, when that dark syrupy stream rises through the funnel, I can’t look away. It’s not elegant. It’s almost too strong, sometimes bitter at the back of the throat, but it wakes you up in a way that feels honest, like cold water to the face.

I don’t measure which I like best. They each serve their purpose, depending on how the air feels, how my thoughts sit in my head. Some mornings demand stillness, others speed, others noise. The tools remain the same—burr grinder, chipped ceramic mug, the kettle with the wobbly base—but the outcome shifts in ways I can’t always name. It’s not about perfection. It’s about the small, repeated act of beginning, of warming the cold cup with my hands and thinking, not about the day ahead, but about the water, the grind, the weight of the press, the sound of the pot climbing toward eruption. And then, for a few quiet minutes, the world pauses in a brown pool at the bottom of a mug.

It’s been three weekends now, lifting, stacking, shifting, and still there’s only a narrow path through the middle of the garage. Not enough for the lawnmower to get through—had to leave it on the driveway overnight last Tuesday. Rain came down around midnight. The blades rusted before first use this spring. Now it’s just another thing to deal with.

Most of what’s piled up has no immediate use. An old bicycle with a bent front wheel. Packing boxes from a kitchen renovation—still full, taped shut as if time might reverse. Christmas lights, tangled in loops so dense they’ve become a single object. You pull one wire and the whole structure shifts, like tectonic plates.

I started at the back wall, the space furthest from the door. Thought logic would dictate that less-used things go farther in. But then the ladder needed to go against that wall, and it’s awkward to drag it forward every time someone needs to change a bulb or clear leaves from the gutter. So now the ladder’s propped near the threshold, blocking half the opening. Ruth pointed that out yesterday. Didn’t say anything sharp, just paused while carrying out a bin of expired cleaning supplies, gave the ladder a look. I moved it later that evening.

Noor helped with the garden tools. Found a pair of pruning shears with the blades fused shut—soaked them in penetrating oil, wire-brushed the rust. They don’t cut clean, but they turn. She lined the handles with grip tape and arranged them upright in a milk crate. Small things like that accumulate and make difference, though it’s hard to notice until later.

Storage solutions end up generating their own clutter. Bought clear bins with labels: “Nails,” “Seeds,” “Extension Cords.” Seemed orderly. Then discovered half the bins didn’t stack evenly—one warped from heat exposure, another got water inside and the label peeled. Now they sit in a teetering column near the washing machine. The label for “Caulk & Adhesives” has smudged into illegibility.

It’s possible that none of this really matters. The garage has always been a half-functional space—part workshop, part holding pen for things that don’t fit elsewhere. But there’s a psychological line, a threshold where inconvenience tips into refusal. You reach a point where you’d rather buy a new drill than dig out the old case from under a tarp and five buckets of unknown screws.

Last night, Priya leaned against the doorframe, watching me try to separate a mass of garden hose. Not asking questions. Just observing. The hose wouldn’t come free. Gave up, tossed it into the “Donate/Dispose” pile. Tomorrow I’ll take it to the drop-off. Maybe. Maybe I’ll just move the pile somewhere else.

The kitchen window above the sink fogged early, condensation beading along the edges of the frame where the seal had long ago failed. Outside, steady rain pressed against the glass, distorting the shape of the oak at the corner of the yard. Indoors, the air carried a faint dampness beneath the scent of tea—earthy and dry—left too long in the pot. A cardboard box, its corners worn soft from storage, came down from the closet shelf. It didn’t slide so much as stagger, dropping awkwardly into waiting hands. There was no fanfare, just the dry rustle of instruction leaflets as the box was set on the low table between two sagging armchairs.

Tiles emerged first—rectangular, forest-green, edged in faded gold. They clattered faintly as they were tipped onto the felt mat someone had laid down earlier, perhaps anticipating the likelihood of spillage. A smaller tray followed, holding wooden pieces in muted blues and yellows. Someone—possibly Kofi—had once filed down the corners of the yellow ones to tell them apart by touch, an adjustment now unevenly remembered by the group. Dice arrived in a chipped ceramic bowl, their pips worn almost smooth on one pair. The second set rolled too easily, a flaw in balance that no one had ever bothered correcting.

Felix took the south side, arranging his tiles into a staggered row, stacking them as if bracing for uneven distribution. Mateo sat cross-legged on the floor, back against the couch, sorting the cards by number despite the lack of formal requirement. Two decks—one for actions, one for terrain—were never fully separated before play began. The rulebook lay open to page six, although no one referred to it until the third round, when a dispute arose over whether a red path could intersect a grey bridge at acute angles.

Midway, the tea was replaced, lukewarm liquid poured into clean mugs without comment. Rain tapped the roof in irregular clusters, sometimes pausing long enough to suggest an end, only to resume with unimpressed consistency. One of the lights overhead buzzed intermittently, its fluorescents dimming whenever the refrigerator cycled on. No one stood to adjust it.

By the time the board filled into its tentative, sprawling pattern—routes connecting, diverging, occasionally dead-ending at the felt’s border—the room had cooled. Socks were retrieved from nearby baskets, pulled on without breaking concentration. Kofi paused to wipe the table’s edge with his sleeve where a smudge of jam had bled from an earlier toast attempt. Play continued, not toward any declared conclusion, but through a tacit agreement that stopping would require more effort than continuing. Pieces shifted. Hands hovered. Somewhere, a clock ticked beneath the sound of rain.

It started raining just after lunch, the kind that settles in without announcement and suggests staying put. The light through the kitchen window turned flat and diffuse, the garden darkening under a steady sheet, so we moved the chairs closer to the table and dug out the wooden box from the shelf above the radiator. Dust on the lid, a stiff hinge—it opened with a small resistance, like something waking.

We played the long game, the one with territories and cards and dice that rattle too loudly on laminate. Petra took the red tokens, Felix the blue, and Mateo, as usual, opted for green even though it meant starting at a slight disadvantage in the southeast quadrant. The rules were cited sporadically, more by habit than necessity. No one kept strict count of trade ratios after the first round, and the neutrality clause between turns four and nine was quietly ignored when it suited.

The radiator popped once, then groaned as the heating cycled. A draft curled under the door from the hall, carrying the damp smell of outerwear left too long on the coatstand. Outside, the rhythm of the rain shifted with little gusts—sometimes urgent, sometimes almost hesitant—while the game unfolded in fits. There was a stretch where nothing much happened, just supply adjustments and cautious repositioning, the kind of lull that makes you glance at the window even if there’s nothing to see.

Tea got made halfway through, weak and lukewarm by the time it reached the table. Petra took a biscuit without looking, her eyes on the northern front. Felix recalculated a border stack after rolling double twos. Mateo didn’t complain, though he usually does. The game wasn’t going anywhere decisive, no clear momentum, just the slow accumulation of moves that could matter in hindsight. And then, for a while, it didn’t seem to matter at all. The board stayed where it was, pieces half-gathered, as the light fell and the rain, still, didn’t ease.

It started raining mid-morning, a slow leak from a gray ceiling that turned everything outside into a smeared watercolor. Beatriz had come over with a puzzle, something cathedral-related, five thousand pieces, but Felix said no — too much like work. That left options. We had three board games between us: one that required strategy, one that required luck, and one that had been a joke gift from a cousin two Christmases ago and nobody had ever properly opened.

Amara unfolded the joke one. It had rules written in rhyming couplets and used a deck of cards with pictures of household disasters. She shrugged. “Could be worse.” The kitchen light buzzed faintly. Rain tapped the skylight like a finger testing glass.

We sat on the floor, backs against the couch, game between us like a campfire. The rules made no sense at first. You rolled dice to avoid consequences, then drew cards to suffer new ones. There was an element of pretending not to care, even though Amara hoarded the tin cat token and Beatriz kept count of bonus points like they mattered.

Felix gave up and switched to the strategy game. He set it up quietly in the corner, moved alone through a wooden empire with heavy inevitability. Every now and then, he’d mutter about betrayal or wheat shortages just loud enough for us to hear. It wasn’t sullen, exactly. More like he needed the silence to think.

Outside, the rain thickened. A car passed too fast, sending up a curtain of water that slapped the side gate. The cat jumped from the windowsill and landed beside me, shaking droplets from her fur before curling into my coat sleeve. I let her stay. Nobody complained.

Amara won the joke game on the third round, mostly because Beatriz misread a card and declared herself mayor of a flooded town. We laughed, but the sort that trails off quickly, tired at the edges. The dice went back in the box. The cat licked a paw.

Beatriz opened the strategy game’s instruction booklet, peered at the diagrams. “Could I play halfway through?” she asked Felix. He nodded without looking up.

Amara got up to make tea. Kettle whistled. She brought back two mugs, left one near the puzzle box — cold now, forgotten. I stirred five spoons of sugar into mine and watched the spoon blur in the dark liquid.

The cat shifted in my sleeve, restless. Rain softened again, not stopping but losing interest. A gap in the clouds gave us ten minutes of uneven light before closing back in.

Felix took a long turn. Beatriz waited, not impatient, just still. Amarateased her about crying over property taxes. Nobody moved to close the curtains. The last of the daylight strained through in gray stripes, one across the board, one across the arm of the couch, barely touching the floor.

I lifted my mug, saw my reflection warped in the surface. Put it down again.

I keep three coffee-makers, which sounds excessive even to me. One lives on the counter, a dented chrome thing that clicks and gurgles like a man clearing his throat after a bad dream. It’s dependable in its way, though it tends to burn the edge of the pot around 7:15 a.m. daily, just as I’m trying to find my keys. The second is French press: a squat glass relic with a chipped knob on top and a plunger that sometimes refuses to descend fully unless I twist it just so—like coaxing a jar lid open with stiff fingers. The third is a little pour-over setup I bought during a brief, feverish interest in precision, the sort of thing that makes you weigh beans and measure water temperature like you’re preparing a chemical analysis instead of breakfast. That one sits mostly idle, a silent reproach on a shelf near the window, next to a dead succulent and some rusty scissors.

I don’t need three. I barely need one. But each method arrives with its own rhythm, its own small betrayal or promise, and in the sticky, unremarkable hours before real daylight, that matters. The automatic machine is for weekdays, when I’m half-asleep and resentful of intent. It asks nothing of me but time and ground beans, the finer grinds clogging its filter the way unresolved arguments clog a conversation. I yell at it once, months ago, when it overflowed during a power flicker. I regretted it immediately, not because it heard me—it didn’t—but because I startled the cat, who has never fully trusted me since.

The French press I use on weekends, when I have an hour to waste and a belief that I might become the kind of person who savors things. The ritual is satisfying: boiling water, coarse grind, starting the timer, stirring with a wooden spoon that smells faintly of old tea. But inevitably, some sludge slips through, and the last sip tastes like mud and commitment. Ruth once told me she loved the French press because it felt “honest.” I nodded, but didn’t say I thought most honesty was overrated. She left a note once, tucked under the sugar bowl: “Better than nothing.” I’m not sure what she meant. I keep it folded in a drawer with expired coupons.

The pour-over I’ve used exactly four times. The last time, Luca came by early, before I’d shaved or decided whether I believed in free will. He watched me pour in slow spirals like a monk performing a sacrament, and said, “Seems like a lot just to stay awake.” He wasn’t wrong. I poured the coffee—light, almost sour—and we sat in silence. He didn’t ask for a second cup. Later, I found the grounds still in the filter, forgotten, dried into a brittle disc. It looked like a fossil.

Now the pour-over gathers dust. The French press sits near the sink, mid-rinse. The machine hums slightly, even when off, a quiet electrical pulse I only notice when the house is otherwise still. I don’t know why I keep all three. Habit, maybe. Or the hope that one of them, someday, will make the right kind of coffee—the kind that tastes like clarity, or like something being explained gently. It hasn't yet. But this morning, the automatic machine didn’t burn the pot. And the cat didn’t look at me sideways. So, I count it as a kind of success—one small cup at a time.

It started with the ladder. Not the kind you lean against a house, but the step kind, the yellow fibreglass one that’s been out there since—well, since whatever season it was when the gutters needed doing. It was blocking the left-side door track, and every time you tried to open the big sliding door, the wheels would jam on the base rung. Been like that for months. You’d just shove harder, or lift the door slightly, or ignore it. Like most things.

The rain had been coming sideways for a week, and the wind got into the seal where the door didn’t quite meet the frame. Water pooled near the lawnmower, which hasn’t started in two seasons. That did it. Time to shift everything. One of those days where the sky is the colour of dishwater and you know you’re not going anywhere anyway.

Started left to right. Dragged the ladder out and stood it upright against the house wall, where it stayed, still leaning slightly, like it’s waiting. Then the bins—yard waste, plastic, whatever. Two were cracked. Just chucked the cracked ones into the back of the hatchback. Deal with them later. The others got cleaned out with a hose, wiped down with a rag that was already greasy.

Found the toolbox. Not the little red one—the old wooden one, the one with the dovetail joints that don’t quite close anymore. It was tucked behind the bicycle with the flat tyre. Lifted it. Heavy. Dropped it onto the concrete and spilled half its contents: rusted screws, a measuring tape with no spring, a pair of pliers missing their rubber grip. Let it sit there. Pick it up later.

There’s a shelf made of cinder blocks and old boards. It sags. On it: paint cans, some opened, some not. One had leaked—white latex gone grey and pitted. Smelled stale. Duct-taped the lid because why not. Moved the unopened ones off to the opposite corner, stacked them somewhat neatly. The rest? Left for now.

Weeded through a plastic tub labeled *Christmas* in marker. Box cutters everywhere. One had tape still on the blade. Found the string lights we never put up last year. Should’ve thrown them out then. Still didn’t. Just shoved them back in, on top of the inflatable snowman, which has a hole near the base. Full circle.

Took a break. Sat on the step. Priya came out with a mug of tea. Didn’t say much. Just handed it over and went back inside. Condensation dripped off the eaves.

Back at it. The freezer—that old upright model we keep for meat and backups—was unplugged and open. Found breadcrumbs, a bag of peas from when the kids were smaller, a foil-wrapped thing with no label. Threw out the food. Left the freezer there. Might need it again someday.

Bins for donations. Made two. One filled fast: jackets too small, tools never used, a set of chipped dishes from the cottage sale. The other—harder to fill. Just odds. A skateboard with one wheel missing. A bag of gravel. A tarp, half-buried.

Aoife came out around four, asked if I’d seen her bike helmet. Found it under a sack of mulch. She nodded, took it, didn’t thank, just walked off. Fair enough.

Ended up sweeping. Pushed dust bunnies the size of cats into corners. Didn’t vacuum. Sweeping was enough. The floor’s still stained. Oil, probably. Or old leaves.

Didn’t finish. Door still rubs. The shelf still sags. But the ladder’s out of the way at least. And you can open the door without lifting it like you’re prying open a jar.

For now.

Yeah, so I finally tackled the garage yesterday. Been meaning to do it since, what, March? Maybe longer. Doesn’t matter. It got to the point where I couldn’t park the car all the way in—half in, half out, like some sad suburban crime scene—so I knew I had to do something.

Started around ten, thinking I’d be done by lunch. Famous last words. By noon I’d just cleared a narrow path from the door to the workbench, and it was all kind of a mess. Boxes marked “Xmas Decor – Fragile” from three moves ago. An old bike with a flat tire and no chain. A stack of magazines someone gave me when I first moved in—the kind with house plants on the cover and articles about “cozy minimalism” I’d never read. And the bins. So many bins. Some full of hardware nobody remembers buying, others half-empty and spilling out twist ties and mismatched screws.

Thao came by around one, saw me sweating in a t-shirt I should’ve thrown out years ago, and just stood in the doorway laughing. “You look like you’re digging a grave,” she said. I told her to go away, but she grabbed a box and started sorting garden stuff—broken terracotta pots, bags of soil with mold on them, a pair of shears rusted shut. We both just stood there at one point, staring at a plastic milk crate full of extension cords knotted together like snakes.

“You think anyone ever just *wants* to reorganize a garage?” she asked.

“Doubt it,” I said. “They just get tired of tripping over stuff.”

We managed to free up one whole shelf, stacked the “keep but not here” stuff near the door—donating, I guess, though no idea when that’ll actually happen. The rest we boxed or tossed. There was this old lamp with a crooked shade, and I swear I’ve seen that thing in three different garages. Not sure whose it was. Still sitting on the curb outside.

Found some weird things, too. A VHS tape labeled “Cabin 2007” — not sure if that’s a vacation or a therapy session. A pair of rollerblades with one wheel missing. And the smell. Oh man. Part damp concrete, part forgotten groceries, part… mouse? No droppings or nests, but you know that faint, sour musk? That’s there. Probably just wet cardboard rotting behind the water heater.

Petra texted at some point asking if I’d seen her yoga mat. I told her it was probably under the pile of drop cloths I hadn’t touched yet. She said, “Then it’s been dead to me for months,” which felt accurate.

By five, the car could fully enter and exit. That was the win. Everything else is still kind of unresolved. The shelves I reorganized this morning are already looking iffy—cans of paint stacked too high, a box of holiday lights shoved sideways. And the lightbulb holder I took down because it was flickering? Still in the same spot on the floor. I moved it six inches. Progress.

Marisol stopped by with a plate of food and stood just inside, peering around like she was entering a disaster zone. “Looks… airier,” she offered. I nodded. She didn’t mention the dust on my face or the fact I was wearing one gardening glove and one winter mitten. Too polite, or maybe just used to me.

I sat on an upside-down bucket for like ten minutes just looking at it all. The space where the old lawnmower used to be (donated? sold? hauled off?) is empty now, just concrete and a faint oil stain. Feels strange. Like the garage doesn’t know what to do with itself.

Might paint the walls tomorrow. Or not. The brush is still in the tray from last spring, hardened into a solid shape I can’t even identify.

The kitchen was still dim when I started, blinds only halfway up. Water measured at 36°C—slightly cooler than ideal, but close enough. Weighed the flour: 500g strong white, a level teaspoon of salt, 7g of dry yeast. Mixed dry, then added 350ml water incrementally. The dough came together quickly, no shaggy stage, which suggests hydration could’ve been marginally higher—perhaps the flour absorbed less than expected due to overnight storage. Hand-kneaded for ten minutes. Surface was clean, bowl scraped once with bench scraper. Left to bulk ferment in a lightly oiled container, covered with a damp cloth.

Checked at 90 minutes: rise of about 50%, sluggish. Room temperature holds at 19°C—may have slowed activity. Punched down, pre-shaped into a loose boule, rested 20 minutes on the counter under a tea towel. Final shape was tight, seam sealed well. Scoring with the lame—first cut hesitant, second cleaner. Dusting of flour sufficient to prevent drag.

Baked in a preheated Dutch oven: 240°C for 20 minutes with lid on, then 220°C for 25 minutes lid off. Crust colour is decent—amber with uneven darkening along one edge, possibly due to oven hot spot. Internal temperature registered 97°C when probed near the base. Crumb is open but not even; larger pockets toward the center, more compact near the crust. Texture is chewy, slightly gummy in the thickest part—may have under-baked by 3–5 minutes.

The loaf sits cooling on the rack. Luca came in halfway through, asked what I was doing. Said it smelled like “the bakery near the station.” He ate a small piece of the heel despite the warning. Aoife took a photo, sent it somewhere. Elena left a note on the counter—something about meetings—didn’t read it all. There’s flour on the tile by the bin. Tea has gone cold. I’ll reheat the kettle, wait for the bread to drop below 50°C before slicing properly. Might try adding a tablespoon of oil next time. Or not.

Repotting the yucca again. It’s like wrestling a grumpy pensioner who refuses to move but insists on complaining about the damp. Last time I did this, three years ago, I swore I wouldn’t bother again unless it actually started leaning toward the light like a drunk. Now it’s half-inched its way to the windowsill edge and nearly took the cat’s tail off when it toppled in a breeze. So, fine. Up you get.

The pot had to be cracked free with a screwdriver. Not elegant. I should’ve taken it outside, but the weather’s that awkward April phase—dry one minute, then a sudden lashing of rain that feels personally offended. So now there’s soil on the floor, on my trousers, in the dog’s ears, probably. The roots were circling the bottom like they’d lost faith in geography and just kept looping, hoping to find an exit that wasn’t there. That part always unsettles me. All that effort, going nowhere. Still, you cut some, tease others, pretend you know what you’re doing.

Amara walked in halfway through and said, “You look like you’re defusing something,” then left without waiting for a reply. She was right, though. Gloves on, knife in hand, brow furrowed at this silent green adversary. I poked at it like it owed me money.

The new pot is heavier, glazed, awkward to lift—one of those I bought because it looked good in the shop and now I regret every time I have to water it. But the yucca seems pleased. Or as close to pleased as a plant can get. It doesn’t wilt, doesn’t drop leaves dramatically. It just endures. I overwatered it once years back and it sulked for months, shedding brown spikes like passive-aggressive confetti. Lesson learned.

Now it’s settled in its new home, lopsided, slightly scorched on one side from last summer’s sun binge, roots trimmed and spread like an old man stretching after a nap. I watered it sparingly. Not out of wisdom, just fear. The last thing I need is a full-blown root rot drama in May.

Outside, the wind’s picking up again. I should’ve waited. I always think these things—repotting, cleaning the gutters, calling my mother—when the weather’s almost nice. Never quite. The dog keeps looking at the yucca like it’s a rival for attention. Maybe it is. It’s been here longer than he has. Older, arguably, in attitude.

I’ll check it tomorrow. Not because it needs me to, but because I like to pretend we’re in this together. It gives nothing back, really. No flowers, no scent, just slow, stubborn survival. Like a lot of things, when you think about it. I wiped the soil off the windowsill. Left the trowel on the radiator. It can dry there. I forgot to wash my hands. They smell like dirt and yesterday’s soap. That’ll do.

Was one of those afternoons where the rain had been at it since morning, slow and constant, not the kind that blows through but the kind that settles in like a guest who forgot to leave. The windows were fogged at the edges, and the gray light made everything feel a few hours later than it was. We pulled the board game down from the top shelf even though it hadn’t been opened in years. The box was dusty, the corners a little dented. I remember Hana gave it a sharp tap on the table to clear the loose grit before flipping it open.

The pieces were all there, more or less. One of the little plastic cars was missing a wheel, but we propped it on its side and pretended it was waiting for repairs. Ruth sat cross-legged with her cardigan sleeves pulled over her fingers. Aoife kept folding and unfolding the money into tiny squares, stacking them beside her like bricks.

We played for maybe two hours, no one really keeping score after the first round. The rules started to blur—someone made up a penalty for humming, then someone else insisted that drawing a certain card meant you had to tell a lie. No one objected. The rain drummed the roof, steady, and we kept passing the dice back and forth like it mattered.

At some point the kettle boiled in the kitchen, forgotten, and let out that long whistle before going quiet. No one got up. We just paused while the sound faded, then Aoife rolled again. Hana laughed at nothing, or maybe at the way her piece was trapped behind two hotels. 

Outside, the streetlight flickered on, not bright but enough to paint a yellow smear across the wet pavement. My foot went numb under the chair. Ruth offered a peppermint from her pocket, the kind wrapped in white paper with a red stripe. I took it, let it sit on my tongue until the sharpness softened. The board still tilted slightly to the left. Aoife was reshuffling the deck with her wrists pressed into the table. The game wasn’t going anywhere. It didn’t need to.

So my bike’s been making this godawful squeak every time I pedal. Not loud, but constant — like a tiny mouse riding on the chainstay and screaming. Started a couple weeks ago, quiet at first, now you could probably hear it from the next street over. I know it’s not just “needs lube,” I’ve been riding through wet stuff and I oil it once a month, sometimes more.

Anyway, finally decided to get on it. Pulled the bike into the kitchen, flipped it over onto the countertop — yeah, yeah, I know — and start inspecting. Wheels? Fine. Chain? Looks okay, but I give it a wipe-down anyway, then a few drops of lube on each link. Spin the pedals, still squeaking. Worse on the downstroke of the right foot.

Check crank arms — wiggle them, no play. Take them off, wipe the square taper and crank insides, reattach and tighten. Squeak’s gone! For like two minutes. Then back, now higher-pitched. Start wondering if it’s pedal-related. Try turning just the right pedal by hand — squeak. Left side? Nothing.

Swap the pedals side to side. Now the squeak follows the right pedal to the left crank. So it’s the pedal. But the bearings feel smooth. Pull it apart on the bench. There’s a little rust ring around the spindle where it meets the body. Wipe it clean, grease the threads, regrease the bearing housing — it’s an older cartridge unit, not sealed like newer ones — reassemble. Put it back on the opposite side.

Back on the bike: squeak’s quieter. Not gone. Ride it around the block — damp pavement, bit of gravel — and it comes back strong after five minutes. Maybe the grease settled wrong? Or maybe the cartridge’s just worn. Luca says sometimes they creak even when they feel fine.

Petra told me once squeaks can travel — sound moves through metal in weird ways. So maybe it’s not even the pedal at all. Could be the bottom bracket. I haven’t touched that in years. But it spins smooth, no grinding. Ingrid had a similar thing — drove her nuts for months — turned out to be the rear derailleur jockey wheel dry, but mine’s been lubed and spins free.

Could it be frame flex? Aluminum seatpost in a steel frame — sometimes they creak where they bind. But I can’t reproduce the noise standing still. Only under pedaling load.

Might take the rear wheel off tomorrow, check the cassette spacers. Or just live with it. It’s not hurting anything. Though the noise is starting to get in my head when I ride. Like it’s judging me.

Repotting a houseplant that’s been in the same container for years can be an act of quiet defiance—yours against the plant’s entrenched resistance. Some specimens, particularly those with dense, fibrous roots or a tendency to interlock tightly with the pot walls, make the process more involved than anticipated. Success requires patience, the right tools, and an understanding of the plant’s structure.

Begin by assessing readiness. Look for signs: roots emerging from drainage holes, water pooling on the surface instead of absorbing, or soil that dries too quickly. If the plant has been in the same container for two or more years, it's likely time. Choose a replacement pot only one to two inches wider in diameter. Going too large can lead to overwatering and root rot, as the soil mass will retain moisture longer than the root system can manage.

Clear a flat, stable work surface near a sink or garden area. Lay down newspaper or an old towel to catch debris. Gather a clean trowel, pruning shears sanitized with alcohol, a small hand fork, and potting mix suited to the plant type—generally a well-draining blend with perlite or pumice. Have a watering can nearby, filled with room-temperature water that’s been sitting for at least an hour.

Remove the plant from its current container by laying it on its side and gently tapping the pot’s rim against a table edge. If resistance persists, run a sterile knife along the inner edge to break the seal. For especially stubborn plants, like a mature pothos or a rootbound peace lily, you may need to squeeze the pot or, as Tomas once did with a fiddle-leaf fig, carefully cut the container away with heavy-duty scissors. Avoid pulling by the stems.

Once free, inspect the root mass. Many long-term specimens will show roots coiled tightly around the base, sometimes circling in on themselves. This girdling can strangle growth over time. Use the hand fork to gently loosen the outer layer, teasing roots apart without tearing. Where roots are thick and woody, pruning shears can make small strategic cuts—no more than a third of the circumference—to encourage outward growth. Discard any dark, mushy sections; these are signs of rot.

Kofi, who keeps a half-dozen monstera in various stages of development, layers the new pot with an inch of fresh mix before positioning the plant. Center it carefully, ensuring the crown sits at the same level it did previously—sometimes slightly higher to allow for settling. Add mix gradually, pressing lightly with your fingers to eliminate air pockets. Do not compact heavily; this restricts water flow and root respiration. Leave about half an inch of space between the soil surface and the pot rim for watering.

Water thoroughly until drainage begins. This settles the mix and hydrates stressed roots. Place the plant in indirect light for at least a week; direct sun at this stage may scorch leaves already coping with transplant shock. Thao notices yellowing tips on her bird’s nest fern after repotting—this usually passes in time.

Monitor moisture closely over the next few weeks. The rearranged root system won’t draw water as efficiently at first. Resist overcompensation.

One neglected spider plant survived seven years in a cracked terracotta, roots circling like old telephone wire. Transplanted into plastic, with a mild root trim and refreshed soil, it unfurled two new shoots within a month. Not every plant rebounds so visibly.

It's not a transformation but a recalibration—silently acknowledged. The scissors go back in the drawer. The trowel rests beside the sink. Unseen, the plant reaches into its new space.

Ugh. Spent way too long wrestling with the fiddle-leaf fig today. Should’ve just left it — it was fine where it was — but no, I had to go poking around because the leaves looked a little droopy. Classic. Always poking.

Turns out it was root-bound something fierce. Like, the roots were circling around the inside of the pot like they’d given up on life and started meditating. I had to literally chisel the thing out with a butter knife. Not proud. Pot ended up cracked anyway. Soil everywhere. Had to sweep the kitchen twice.

Ruth popped her head in and went, “You’re making that so hard,” which, thanks, very helpful. She didn’t stick around to help, obviously. Just commented and left. People.

Anyway, once I got it free, it sort of unfolded like a nervous accordion. Felt weirdly personal, like I’d seen something I shouldn’t have. Plopped it into the new pot — bigger, ceramic, looks nice but probably too top-heavy now — and tried to press fresh soil around it. But the root ball wouldn’t sit right. Kept tipping. Ended up having to lift it out again and shave off some of the outer roots. Felt kind of brutal, like giving it a haircut with garden shears. I apologized to it. Not joking.

Watered it in with that weird-smelling rainwater I’d been saving in the bucket. Hope that wasn’t a mistake. It’s leaning to the left now. Maybe it’ll straighten out? Probably not. Devon says these things “go through phases,” whatever that means. He also says, “Plants want to live,” which is nice in theory, but doesn’t help when you’re knee-deep in compost and regret.

Now it’s just sitting there in the corner, looking judged. Light’s not great this time of day. Should’ve waited till morning. Next time. If there is a next time. I mean, it’s just a plant, right?

Wait — did I forget to clean the saucer?

Repotting a stubborn houseplant is not a horticultural act so much as an act of negotiation with a slightly passive-aggressive roommate. The ficus in question—call it Gerald, because it has the quiet resentment of a man who’s been wronged unfairly—has lived in the same terracotta pot since long before anyone in this apartment learned how to fold a fitted sheet. It sits in the corner by the north-facing window, casting judgmental glances at houseguests and occasionally shedding a leaf like a dropped mic.

I’d been putting it off for months, of course. Not because I care about continuity or emotional bonds with foliage—though there is something vaguely guilt-inducing about exhuming something that’s quietly suffered your neglect for years—but because repotting Gerald always feels less like gardening and more like crisis management. Soil goes everywhere, roots resist, and halfway through, you’re knee-deep in organic matter, muttering to yourself about "just trying to help."

This time, I waited until a Saturday when the light was flat and the rain hadn’t yet started. A good day for soil work, Beatriz had said once, mysteriously, as if such days were coded into the weather patterns. I found the new pot—larger, plastic, the color of weak tea—and laid out newspaper like I was preparing for a crime scene. The trowel felt wrong in my hand, like I’d picked up someone else’s pen.

Gerald did not go quietly. Turning the pot upside down and whacking it—standard procedure—did nothing. It held fast, like a dog that’s decided not to move. I had to slide a butter knife around the edge, whispering reassurances I didn’t believe. When it finally came free, the root ball was a tight, matted fist, circling itself like a nervous habit. It looked offended.

I gave it a rough loosening with my fingers, which felt unnecessarily intimate, and lowered it into the new pot. The extra soil sat in a bag on the floor, waiting. There was a moment—just one—where everything hung in balance: the plant, the pot, the unspoken agreement between us that this might not end well.

Then, a small sound. A crack in the old soil, maybe. Or the window creaking in the wind. It wasn’t a sign of anything, not really. Just the house doing what houses do. I watered it lightly and left it there, damp and unimpressed, staring at the wall.

After a while, I came back and moved it a few inches to the right. We’ll see.

The kitchen light comes on at 7:08, not bright but enough to catch flour motes drifting past the window edge. It’s overcast, the kind of grey that presses low on the rooftops, so the overhead bulb stays on. There’s a mixing bowl already on the counter, chipped near the rim, and a clean towel folded beside it. The recipe—written on a slip of paper tucked behind a knife block—calls for 500 grams of flour, 360 milliliters of water, 10 grams of salt, and 200 grams of starter. The starter is in a jar, lid slightly ajar, resting on a saucer to catch any overflow. It’s bubbly, with a faint vinegar tang rising when the lid’s removed.

Water from the kettle is cooled to hand-warm, not quite body temperature. Too hot kills the culture. Too cold slows it down. The starter goes in first, followed by water, stirred with a fork until mostly combined. Flour is added in two stages, incorporating with a rubber spatula before hands take over. The dough starts sticky, clumping in strands between fingers. Salt is sprinkled on last, worked in through folding and pressing. Once the mass begins to hold together—no longer adhering in patches to the bowl—kneading begins on a floured board.

Twenty minutes of intermittent work. Fold, press, turn. Rest five minutes. Repeat. The dough grows smoother, resists less. Windowpane test near the end: a small pinch stretched thin enough to let light through without tearing. When it passes, it’s lowered back into the bowl, covered with the towel, and left to bulk ferment. Ambient temperature matters—today it’s about 21°C—so the bowl sits near the window, not in direct air from the vent.

Two hours in, the dough has risen unevenly, domed toward the south side. First fold: damp hands lift one edge, stretch it gently upward, then fold over the center. Repeat on opposite side, then rotate ninety degrees and do it again. The surface tautens slightly. Towel back in place.

By 10:15, Tomas comes in, says nothing, fills the kettle. He pours a cup, leans on the counter. The dough is 50% larger now, airy but still structured. It’s shaped into a round with cupped hands, seam sealed underneath, then lowered into a floured proofing basket. Another towel draped over top.

Outside, a siren passes, then fades. The oven remains off. Not yet. The bread still has time.

Took me most of Saturday to start in on the garage. Not the whole thing—don’t be ridiculous—but the corner near the lawnmower, the one that’s been swallowed up by old plant pots, a broken step stool, and three bins full of who-knows-what. You know how it goes. Stuff piles up not because you’re lazy, exactly, just because there’s never a good time to deal with it, and suddenly, six years have passed and you’re tripping over a child’s balance bike you thought you’d donated.

I started by dragging everything out into the driveway. Not heroic work, just grunting and pulling and trying not to knock over the neighbour’s bins, which are parked dangerously close to our shared fence. Ruth came out at one point, looked the heap over, and said, “Wow. That’s worse than I remembered,” then went back inside without offering to help. Fair enough. She’s not the one who keeps promising to fix the hedge trimmer.

There’s no system to how we’ve stored anything. Garden tools next to leftover paint tins. A set of winter boots shoved behind a bag of potting soil that’s now a hard, cracked brick. I found two of Mateo’s school art projects in a plastic sack—something about volcanoes, on poster board, with actual glitter. Took a photo and sent it to him. He texted back: “Please burn it.” I put it on the keep pile.

Most of the bins were sealed shut with years of dust. One had a tangle of Christmas lights, the kind with the big bulbs. I remember putting them up after the tree came down in the storm of ’19. Still work, actually. Another held a half-used bag of birdseed and two nesting wrens. They flew off when I lifted the lid. I left the bin open for now—figured they might want to come back, or maybe they’ve moved on. Hard to say.

Sorted things into little zones: things to throw, things to donate, things that belong in the house, things that belong in the garage but have no business being in that exact spot. No grand plan. Just boxes on the ground and a lot of heavy breathing.

Petra stopped by with a jug of iced tea. We sat on the curb for ten minutes, not talking much, just watching clouds move. She has a clean garage. Always has. Didn’t mention it, though. Good friend.

I didn’t finish. Didn’t expect to. But the door closes now. That’s something.

It’s 7:14. The kettle’s whistling isn’t so much a signal as an accusation, like it knows I’m already behind. I’m not a morning person, not really, but I can’t not start here—kettle, water, coffee bin already half full from yesterday’s second round. The kitchen light flickers on the stainless steel sink, upright and cold. I’ve got the French press sitting on the counter from last night, rinsing out the last bitter residue. Marisol uses it every day—says it’s the only way she gets volume, enough to last until noon. Feels rustic, she claims. To me, it feels like a chore. But I go through the motions.

Grind too coarse, water just off the boil, stir once after pouring. Let it sit four minutes. Press slowly, like you're afraid of what’s underneath. Nine out of ten times it’s fine. Sometimes muddy. Sometimes warm and flat, like the coffee gave up halfway. Still drinkable. Always drink/able.

Then there’s the pour-over—what Felix swears by. Had to buy the cone, the paper filters, the little gooseneck kettle with the curved neck that makes you feel like a chemistry student. He brought it over last winter, set it up like a demonstration. “It’s about control,” he said, weighing the beans before grinding. Seriously—grams, not scoops. Water heated to exactly 96°C, which he checked on a digital thermometer. Poured in concentric circles like he was lacing a boot. I admit, it tasted good. Bright, even. Said the method “lets the coffee speak.” I didn’t argue. But between the gear and the ceremony, it’s not something I reach for when I’m half-awake and hunting for caffeine like it’s loose change under the couch.

Still, once a month, maybe, I do it. Not for the taste so much as to remind myself I can. That I haven’t entirely surrendered to convenience. Clean cone, rinse filter, bloom the grounds with a bit of water—watch them puff up like rising dough. Then the slow spiral, until the cup fills with that clear amber brew. Never strong, never dark. Like coffee after a yoga retreat. Delicate. Unbothered. I sip it slowly, even though I don’t have time.

And then there’s the electric drip. The machine that’s been in the back cupboard for two years, dragged out after the apartment renovation. Black, boxy, forgotten until the French press cracked and Marisol moved out. Now it’s mine. Set it up the night before—filter, grounds, water reservoir filled. Wake up, press button. Twenty seconds later, the gurgle begins. It sounds like something digesting.

Doesn’t taste amazing. Sometimes metallic, sometimes hollow in the middle. But it’s fast. Consistent enough. Makes a full pot without drama. I don’t like admitting it, but I’ve come to appreciate the indifference of it—no ritual, no waiting, no need to “listen” to the coffee. It just makes coffee.

Today, it’s the drip. I need to leave in eleven minutes. Felix sent a text: “Try the new beans with the pour-over.” I glance at the shelf—the bag sits there, unopened. Later, maybe. I pour a cup from the still-warm pot, blow on it, take a sip. It’s hot. It’s dark. It’s what I need.

The mug’s half empty already. I hadn’t noticed. Outside, someone’s dragging a trash can across pavement. A dog barks. I refill the cup, grab my coat, and

So. There I was. 6:47 a.m., the sky still undecided on whether it was committed to light, and I’d gone and done it — committed to sourdough. Not the lazy one where you start the night before, no, the full dramatic ritual: levain refreshment, autolyse, coil folds on the hour like some kind of nervous tic. Beatriz says I do it to punish myself. Maybe she’s not wrong.

The starter bubbled with an enthusiasm I couldn’t match. I poked it. It didn’t poke back. Fair enough. Measured out the flour — yes, the “bread flour,” the one I bought three months ago and haven’t used because it’s expensive and I’m weirdly intimidated by it — and tried not to spill. Failed. A fine dust settled over the counter like a light snowfall in a tiny, abandoned village. I left it there. The universe didn’t collapse.

Petra once told me you can tell how the dough will behave by how it feels under your fingers. I stood there, hands covered in wet flour paste, trying to interpret texture like I was reading tea leaves. It was… sticky. Also, sort of shaggy. Was that “shaggy” in a good way? Did it mean it was developing gluten, or just that I hadn’t added enough water? Or too much? I poured a little more water in. Then regretted it. There’s no undo in bread.

Somewhere around the third coil fold, Tomas walked in, bleary, muttering about the “weird smell.” I said it was bread. He said it smelled like “something died in an old towel.” I pointed out that sourdough starter is literally fermented flour and water that’s supposed to smell like a damp basement where a science project went wrong. He poured coffee and left. I took this as moral support.

The dough sat in its bowl, covered with a damp tea towel I’d stolen from the linen closet. It sagged. Then, slowly, it didn’t. I checked it. Not much. Checked again twenty minutes later. Still not much. Then, just when I’d given up and started mentally reheating a frozen pita, it looked… different. Puffed up. Wobbly in a proud way. Like it had finally decided to participate.

I shaped it. Or attempted to. It slid around like a wet octopus. I flour-dusted. I cursed softly. A little too much flour? Too little tension? Who knows. I tucked the edges the way the videos do, which in practice felt like trying to fold a very soft pillow into a briefcase. The boule looked more like a deflated football, but it was in the banneton now, resting, doing its thing. I covered it. Told it I believed in it. Me and the dough, both pretending we had our lives together.

Preheated the oven with the Dutch oven inside, because apparently metal must suffer before it bakes bread. Settled in with a book I wasn’t reading, mostly staring at the rising lump under the towel, wondering if time was passing differently for it. Bread time vs. human time. It’s not the same. I swear, it breathes slower.

When I finally turned it out, the dough wobbled with quiet dignity. I slashed it with a knife that definitely wasn’t as sharp as it should’ve been — the cut frayed like a torn shirt — and dropped it into the scalding pot. Literally dropped. A little splash of steam hit my wrist. Fine. We were even.

Now it’s baking. The house smells less like death and more like something trying. Hard to say if it’ll be good. Might be dense. Might have a crater where the center collapsed. Might be amazing. But it’s in the oven. That counts for something.

The sky’s properly light now. Birds are yelling. Coffee’s cold. I’m still in yesterday’s socks.

Could’ve just bought a loaf, sure.

But here we are.

It started, as it often does, with the quiet hum of the kettle on the stove. No rush. Just the slow climb of steam, the first crack of pressure. French press, this morning. Coarse grind, always coarse, no fines sneaking through. Pour just off the boil, wait four minutes, press slow—like squeezing a sponge underwater. Kofi used to say the secret was in the plunge: steady, no jerking at the end. I never believed him, not really, but I do it anyway. Habit. Or maybe I do believe him. The cup’s thick, earthy, a little oily on the rim. Comfort.

Later, at Dmitri’s, an electric drip. His machine’s older, beige, probably his father’s, and it gurgles like it’s dying. But the coffee’s clean. Filter paper, medium grind, water that takes too long. He leaves the pot on the burner too long. I don’t say anything. We talk about the neighbour’s dog, about how the stairs squeak near the top. The coffee turns flat after ten minutes, but we don’t pour it out. We drink it anyway, in small sips, lukewarm.

Then, tonight—because I stayed up too late again—Luca’s gift. The little stovetop percolator. He calls it “retro,” but it’s just what his grandmother used. Aluminum, dented on one side. Water in the bottom, coffee in the basket, not too tight. Flame low. The gurgle starts soft, builds. I watch it through the window on the side—dark stream rising, pulsing. When it turns thin, golden, I pull it off. A little bitter, always a little bitter, but strong enough to pull me back to the room, to the clock, to the cold floor under my feet.

I don’t know if any of it’s right. I don’t think it matters. It’s the doing—measure, heat, wait—that keeps the edges from fraying. The taste is just an excuse.

The squeak started on a Tuesday, though I didn’t notice right away. It wasn’t urgent, just there—like someone gently clearing their throat behind me whenever I turned left. At first, I blamed the pavement. Then the wind. Eventually I had to admit it was the bike. My bike. The one with the slightly lopsided basket where I carry library books and the occasional bruised apple.

I don’t hate bicycles, but I don’t romance them either. I don’t know the names of parts beyond “wheel” and “handlebar,” and I’ve never once adjusted a derailleur in my life. Still, the sound wore me down. It wasn’t loud, but it was insistent, like a relative who shows up uninvited and stays for three days. I could’ve taken it in to the shop, but something about dragging the thing across town for what felt like a complaint annoyed me. So instead, one damp afternoon, I hauled it into the garage and stood there, hands on hips, like a detective at a minor crime scene.

I sprayed oil on what I assumed was the hub. The squeak paused, then returned with spite. I read a paragraph on a phone screen about chain tension and felt immediately out of my depth. Marisol walked by, paused, gave the tire a spin, shrugged. “Could be the brake pad,” she said, though she sounded more tired than certain. That sent me down a new tunnel of doubt. I took the front wheel off—more confidently than perhaps I should’ve—and wiped the rim with a rag. The brake pad looked innocent enough. I oiled again. Listened. Tried not to feel foolish crouching there with a greasy rag and misplaced hope.

At some point, probably after the third reassembly, the noise stopped. Or maybe it just blended back into the general hum of things—the garage door, a distant mower. I didn’t celebrate. Just wheeled it out, gave it a test push. Silence. Temporary, maybe. But for now, the bike and I are on neutral terms.

I don’t know how it got so bad. At some point, the garage stopped being a place for the car and started absorbing everything else. There was a bike with a flat tire leaning against a stack of garden pots, a busted shelving unit with a tarp slung over it like a sad fort, and an old stroller with chewed-through straps that the dog once used as a teething toy. It looked less like storage and more like a museum of things we couldn’t quit.

I finally started on Saturday—no plan, just frustration. After pulling the car into the driveway and turning the ignition off, I just stayed seated for a minute, looking at the mess. It wasn’t dangerous, really. Just heavy with intent. Like all the things were waiting for me to care.

I took out a single box and opened it. Inside: Christmas lights from three houses ago, tangled so bad I didn’t bother untangling them. A pottery candle holder Yuki made in high school, the paint now chipped, one corner cracked. I remembered her handing it to me at dinner, proud and nervous, and for a second I almost put it back in the box. But I set it on the workbench instead. On top of the driveway heater that doesn’t work.

I found Owen’s old snowboard, which surprised me. He’d outgrown it, but it felt like a relic, a reminder of winters when we still drove upstate together. Now it was just another thing with a warped edge and faded base. I propped it against the wall. Maybe it could hold up the tarp.

Midway through, Luca wandered out in his socks, squinting in the light. “You crying?” he asked. I wasn’t—just sweating and annoyed. But I rubbed my face and maybe there was something sticky near my eyes. He handed me a glass of water and didn’t say anything else. Just stood there for a minute, looking at the mess, then the things I’d saved, then back at me.

“You want help?” he asked.

I said maybe in a bit. For now, I just kept sorting.

The shadows were getting long.

Reorganising a garage is never just reorganising a garage. It’s more like archaeology funded by guilt. You start with good intentions—maybe a broom, a dust mask you’ll use twice—and soon enough, you’re knee-deep in a broken kayak stand, five bags of “miscellaneous screws,” and the ghost of a garden hose from a previous relationship with lawn care.  

First, clear a path. Not all the way—real people don’t do that. Just enough to get between the lawnmower and the stack of expired camping gear. Everything gets grouped: things you might use, things you’ll definitely use “next summer,” and things that are clearly junk but feel emotionally loaded (a deflated soccer ball Hana kicked into the neighbour’s yard once). Be firm. The ball stays.  

Shelving helps, if it’s already there. If not, don’t buy any. Nobody needs more hardware until they’ve dealt with what they’ve got. Pegboards are fine for tools, though only about 30 percent of what you hang will ever be retrieved. Labeling? Optional. Optimistic.  

Bins are your friend. Clear ones, so you can lie to yourself about remembering what’s inside. Resist the urge to sort by colour. This isn’t a craft store. “Gardening,” “car,” “miscellaneous projectiles”—these are legitimate categories.  

Devon once tried vacuum-sealing old blankets. They inflated by June. Ingrid keeps a bucket for “things to decide about later.” It’s now a permanent fixture near the water heater.  

You’ll find things you forgot: a spare bike lock with no key, a set of wind chimes that annoyed everyone, the receipt for a ladder you no longer own. Some of it goes. Some gets tucked back into shadows.  

By evening, you’ll have lost three hours and gained a slightly better sense of where the extension cord lives. The garage won’t be transformed. It’ll just be slightly more honest. You can close the door without pressing your shoulder into a wheelbarrow. That’s the victory. The rest is weather and time.

Okay, so the bike. Right. Every time I ride it, that squeak gets louder. It’s not even a smooth squeak — more like a series of little groans whenever I pedal, like the bike is judging me. Took me forever to finally do something about it. Not like it’s dangerous or anything, but it’s embarrassing, and also, I don’t know, bugs me.

I mean, I had no clue where to even start. Thought maybe oil? But oil where? So I asked Tomas, and he just said “chain” like it was obvious, which I guess it is, but still. He showed me real quick with his own bike — just a few drops on each link, let it run through while turning the pedals backward. Seemed easy enough.

But then mine still squeaked after that. Like, slightly less, but still there, especially when I stand up to pedal. So I fiddled with it more. Took the chain off to clean it better, which was messy. Got grease on my sweatshirt, which Zainab pointed out later with that I-told-you-so look. Fine, fine.

Then I started wondering if it was the pedals? Or the bottom bracket? Noor said sometimes water gets in the ball bearings and they grind. So now I’m overthinking it and reading random forum posts at 11 PM, people talking about freewheels and crank arms and stuff that sounds like another language.

Went back out this morning and just stared at it for a bit. Sprayed a little more oil where the pedal connects, turned the crank a bunch of times. Rode down the street — seemed quieter. Or maybe I just imagined it because I wanted to. Hard to tell. One squeak every five revolutions now? Improvement?

Still want to take the whole thing apart, but I don’t even know what tools I’d need. And what if I can’t get it back together? Tomas said once you start, you don’t stop till it’s done. But also, he wrecked his brother’s derailleur like that.

Second time I grease it, it’ll probably be smoother. Or maybe not. Doesn’t matter. Just want to ride without sounding like a haunted shopping cart. Took it around the block again. Quiet for a bit. Then — squeak.

Ingrid  
So I finally broke down and bought that new drip machine Beatriz was going on about. Not the fancy one with the app, just the basic stainless thing with the little window. Still pretty overkill for me, honestly. But it’s been sitting on the counter for a week now and I keep defaulting to the French press because I can’t figure out how to stop it from gurgling so loud. Woke Sanjay up yesterday when I hit start at 6:45. Kinda mortifying.

Beatriz  
LOL of course. That gurgle is half the charm. Gets louder the older it gets. Just embrace it. But yeah, French press still rules for weekends. Takes forever but the coffee tastes… louder somehow? Like it’s doing more work. I use my parents’ old one—blue glass, chipped at the rim. Feels more authentic than that shiny machine Ingrid’s got, no offense.

Sanjay  
Speaking of loud… my stupid AeroPress just cracked. Not all the way through, but there’s a hairline thing near the seal. Been using the same one since college. Used to take it camping with that little metal filter that always clogged. Now I just sit at the table watching the sun come up, pushing down like a maniac. Feels like a ritual? Except today the coffee tasted like cardboard again. Might’ve left the beans too long on the counter.

Ingrid  
Cardboard is the worst. Did you grind too fine? I always mess that up with the AeroPress. Once I did that and it was like drinking sludge. Had to filter it through a paper towel, which honestly just ruined everything.

Beatriz  
Grind size fixes everything. Or ruins. I swear, the French press is forgiving. Burnt beans? Still drinkable. Old coffee? Still warm. AeroPress is like that one friend who critiques your socks.

Sanjay  
Yeah, it's judgmental. But satisfying when it works. Just not today. Still better than that cheap pod thing my sister uses. Tastes like wet newspaper wrapped in plastic. Don’t even know how she can stand it.

Ingrid  
My cousin has one. Uses the same pod for two cups. Just pulls it out, flips it over, puts it back in. Told her that’s not how it works but she just shrugged and said, “It’s still warm.” Like that’s the goal?

Beatriz  
As long as it’s hot, right? Honestly, I’ll take lukewarm if the flavor’s there. Spent ten years microwaving old coffee until I realized I could just make a new pot. Small victories.

Sanjay  
I still microwave. Don’t judge.

Beatriz  
Never. We all have our shames. Mine is adding sugar to espresso. Don’t tell anyone.

So Saturday, right. Kind of a last-minute thing. Thao woke me up at like 7:30 saying, “We said we’d go this weekend,” which—okay, fair, we did. But I’d already started reading and was hoping to coast into brunch and just let it fizzle. Anyway, we packed the usual stuff: water, some crackers, jerky, a ziplock of roasted chickpeas (don’t knock it), and my old map case with the laminated trail printout I got from that PDF thing. Thought we were doing the ridge trail? Everyone says it’s easy, has those views? Supposedly clear markers?

Got to the trailhead by nine-thirty, parked near the ranger board. Didn’t actually check in—Kofi’s the one who always does that, but he bailed last minute with something about his nephew’s science fair. We saw a few other cars, so figured we weren’t alone out there. Started off fine. First 45 minutes, nice shade, little stream crossing, birds going nuts in the trees. We even saw a deer, or at least Thao did—she’s convinced it was standing there looking at us, I just saw brown and then gone.

Then the trail forked. No sign. Like, nothing. Just two paths, one narrower, curving left around a rock formation, the other wider but sloping down into this thicker brush. The printout mentioned a fork? Vaguely? I think? But didn’t say left or right. We argued for a minute—Thao wanted to go left, said it looked more “maintained.” I wasn’t sure. But we went left anyway.

That’s when it got weird. Less foot traffic. Roots everywhere. Had to duck under branches, then the path got rocky and steep. Checked the map a few times, but honestly, after five minutes, I wasn’t even sure where we were on it. No landmarks matched. Still thought we were okay—figured it would loop back or intersect with something else.

Then the cloud cover came in. Dropped the temperature quick. We’d taken off jackets at the start, but now Thao was shivering in her thin windbreaker. Kept saying, “Are we going in circles?” Every few minutes. I kept saying no. Mostly to reassure myself.

Hit a dead end at this weird gully—riverside, but not on the map—and had to backtrack like 20 minutes. At one point, we heard voices? Thought we were saved. Ran toward them. Turned out to be echoes off the rock wall—some hikers way up on a ledge we couldn’t reach.

Finally, after what felt like two hours off-course, we broke into this open meadow. Felt like we’d time-jumped—sun came out, wildflowers everywhere. There was a single blue ribbon tied to a tree. Thank God. Followed it, and within ten minutes, we hit a proper trail junction, the actual ridge route.

Never been so happy to see a stupid yellow marker.

We didn’t even make it to the viewpoint. Just turned back toward the car, kind of quiet. Thao kept twisting her ankle on the rocks. I dropped the chickpeas somewhere. Probably when I was arguing about the fork.

Back in the parking lot, someone was handing out free granola samples. We took two each. Didn’t even care if they were weird flavors.

Took me three weekends to figure out my bicycle’s goddamn squeak. Not the pedals, not the chain — that was the first lie I told myself. “Lube the chain,” I muttered, squirting that thin grease everywhere, watching it fling off onto the garage wall like something diseased. No change. The noise came on every rotation: a high-pitched whine, then a groan, like the bike was auditioning for a haunted house.

I started riding it anyway. Took it out in the mornings before work, pedaled through the neighborhood with one ear tuned to the sound, trying to isolate it. Elena, who cycles to the community garden every Thursday, leaned over one day while I was fiddling with the rear tire. “Bearings?” she said. Didn’t wait for a reply. She never does.

Bearings. Right. I Googled that, of course. Found a video where a guy in a well-lit garage pulled apart a bottom bracket with tools that looked expensive. I didn’t have those. What I had was a set of wrenches from a Christmas years ago, a rag, and a can of degreaser I’d bought for something else entirely — maybe the patio hinges.

Next Saturday, I flipped the bike upside down on the driveway. Took the wheels off. Inspected the chainrings. Nothing looked loose, nothing visibly rusted. I tugged at the crank arms. One gave a tiny wiggle. Not much, but enough to make me pause. I unscrewed the bolt on that side, pulled the arm off, and nearly dropped it into a puddle. Underneath: grime, old grease turned black, a few flecks of metal. Not good.

I cleaned what I could. Used the degreaser, got my fingers filthy. Let it dry. Tried to reassemble. Bolt wouldn’t seat right. Felt cross-threaded. I cussed. Sat back on my heels. Bike upside down like a beached insect.

Monday night, Priya called. We hadn’t spoken in ages. She was moving out of the city. Talked about logistics, storage units, freight. I mentioned the bike. “Oh,” she said, “my brother used to rebuild them. Said eighty percent of squeaks are the crank or the pedals. And people always ignore the pedals.”

Pedals. I hadn’t even checked the pedals.

Next day, after work, I went out with a flashlight. Underside of the pedal axle — rust. One side was dry as bone, the thread corroded. I soaked it in degreaser, let it sit. Used a toothbrush. Took it apart carefully. Inside: more gunk, a shattered rubber seal. I didn’t have a replacement seal. Used a bit of electrical tape wrapped tight, regreased the spindle, screwed it back in. Felt stiff. Rode it around the cul-de-sac.

The noise was different. Not gone. But changed — lower, less insistent. Felt like progress. Or maybe just fatigue. I don’t know.

Last night, I took a longer route. Down past the school, across the bridge, through the industrial strip where the streetlights flicker. The sound’s still there, a soft complaining on every revolution. Not as bad. I’ve gotten used to it, maybe. Or it’s gotten used to me.

Could take it to the shop around the corner. The guy there nods a lot, wears greasy gloves, doesn’t say much. But that feels like giving up. Or paying for something I should be able to do. Doesn’t matter. I keep riding. It’s just a squeak.

It started with the sound. A high-pitched, rhythmic whine that cut through the quiet of the bike path every time I turned a crank. Not constant — just after the first few minutes, like the bike needed to warm up before it could complain properly. I’d glance around, half-convinced people could hear it. They probably could. It wasn’t loud, but it was sharp, insistent, the kind of noise that refuses to stay in the background.

I pushed it off for weeks. Stupid, really. Just a squeak, not a death rattle. I tightened the chain once, fiddling with a wrench borrowed from Mateo, who shrugged and said something about lube. I remember him spraying something black and greasy on his own chain, making a face at the smell. I didn’t bother getting oil. The noise came back in three days.

Eventually, I stopped ignoring it. Something about riding past the same patch of cracked pavement every morning, hearing that same note rise and fall with my pedals — it became personal. I walked into the bike shop near the library, asked for “something for a squeaky chain.” The guy behind the counter didn’t even look up. Held out a small bottle with a needle tip. Ten dollars. I passed him a ten, didn’t ask questions.

Back at the apartment, I leaned the bike against the railing on the back step. Dribbled the oil along the chain, careful near the derailleur, though I wasn’t sure why. Turned the pedals backward by hand. The sound changed — less squeak, more wet shuffle. I wiped the excess with an old rag, the kind that lives in the kitchen drawer with the twist-ties and dead batteries.

Took it for a spin later that afternoon. The noise wasn’t gone, not exactly. It had shifted. Softer. Slightly lower, like it was clearing its throat instead of protesting. Rode past the school, up the hill near the overpass. Each rotation felt different — not perfect, but quieter, less strained. I passed Hana walking her dog. She waved. I nodded, kept pedaling. The chain made a small, lubricated click. Then nothing. Then, after a long stretch, a faint echo of the old sound — not gone, just waiting. I didn’t mind so much this time.

It’s been a week of stubbed fingers and soil under my nails, still gritty no matter how I scrub. The fiddle-leaf fig in the parlor—old thing, been with me since before the balcony garden folded—decided it was done with the same cracked terracotta pot I’d been telling myself was “charming.” One strong wind, and it nearly toppled. Marisol glanced at it during tea and said, without looking up from stirring her cup, “That one’s had enough.” Simple as that.

So I pulled out the tarp, set up shop on the back step. The roots had wound through the drainage hole like they were trying to escape. I had to tap the sides, twist it like coaxing a jar lid free. When it finally gave, it made a sound like wet leather pulling apart. Dug my fingers in—carefully, I tell myself, though there was a split in the index finger already, still fresh from the kitchen drawer mishap last Tuesday. The plant didn’t make it easy. It wasn’t just rooted; it was fixed, as though it had declared its place in the world and no amount of polite encouragement would change its mind.

Priya passed by, walking her dog, paused at the gate. “Refusing retirement?” she asked, nodding at the mess. I couldn’t help laughing. She waved and kept on. Kofi came later, carrying that wooden tray he’s always fixing, offered me one of those hard candies from his pocket—peppermint, the kind that tastes faintly of wax. We didn’t talk much, just watched me wrestle the roots with a spoon, teasing apart what I could without breaking them. He said something about how things once set rarely like being shifted, even when it’s better for them. Left it at that.

The new pot is wider, shallow, glazed on the inside. I mixed the soil—half old, half new—with a bit of perlite I found behind the furnace. Didn’t measure. Just filled it, tucked the roots in like I was folding a child into bed. Watered it slow. You can’t rush that part, not after such a strain. It sat there for hours, drooping slightly, then straightened when the light changed. Maybe it was just my eyes playing tricks.

There’s a quiet after moving a plant. Not peace, not exactly. More like the air shifts, aware something’s been changed. The leaves catch the sun differently. I keep glancing over, half expecting it to say something. I know better, of course. But still. It feels like we both held our breath, and now it’s safe to exhale. For now.

Rain all morning. Grey light coming through the blinds, same as yesterday and the day before. By afternoon it eased a bit, just a steady hush on the pavement. Tomas came over with a cardboard box under his arm, the corner a little softened from the damp. Didn’t say much, just shrugged off his coat and kicked his shoes into the hallway.  

We pushed the coffee table closer to the sofa, made space on the rug. The game inside turned out to be one of those old family ones—yellowed rules, a spinner in the middle, little plastic figures shaped like animals. Noor used to have one like it, years back. I don’t remember the name. We dug out tokens from the closet—dice in a chipped cup, a deck of worn cards from somewhere else entirely.  

It started quietly, just the sound of the spinner wobbling and clicking. We didn’t keep score properly. Tomas kept forgetting whose turn it was. I didn’t mind. There was something steady about going through the motions, moving pieces a few spaces, drawing cards that told us to go back or forward or do nonsense like "howl like a dog." We laughed once, not big, but real—the kind that aches a little if you let it keep going.  

The light kept changing. Clouds shifted, then settled again. A car sloshed by every ten minutes or so. The radiator clunked and wheezed like it always does when the heat kicks in.  

Noor came in around five, shook her boots by the door, and sat cross-legged beside the game, watching for a while without joining. She picked up a card, read it silently, placed it back face down. “You remember this one?” she asked Tomas. He nodded. She said nothing else, just stayed there, rubbing her palms on her jeans.  

We stopped playing somewhere past the middle. The board still sitting there, half-finished. No one bothered to put it away. Tomas stood up, stretched, said he should go. I walked him to the door. The rain had picked up again, thin and constant.  

Back in the room, Noor was flipping the spinner. It spun longer than it should have, wobbling more each turn until it dropped flat. She looked at me but didn’t ask anything. I sat down. The rug was warm under my feet. Outside, the streetlight flickered on.

It started easy enough—just the three of us, Noor and Petra and me, boots already scuffed from past hikes, setting out with backpacks full of apples, water, and a vague plan to follow a loop trail back to the river crossing. The sky was pale, the kind of cool gray that promises dry air without sunburn. We were quiet for a while, stepping over roots and broken branches, letting the rhythm of walking do the talking.

Somewhere around what we thought was the second junction, we took a left. There was no real marker, just a faint trail that veered off. Petra hesitated, said something about the map looking different, but Noor insisted it was shorter. I remember that—the tone of voice, the small urgency of someone who doesn’t want to turn back. So we went.
