The trail narrowed. Then it wasn’t really a trail. We passed through thickets of saplings so tight they snagged at our sleeves. The air smelled wet—not from rain, but from soil and decay, the kind of dampness that seeps into your shoes without you noticing. Then the trees changed. Not pine anymore, but something taller and more silent, with bark that peeled in long curls. We stopped checking the map. It felt pointless, like reading a book in the dark.

Petra found a rusted can half-buried in moss. She held it up like a joke, but no one laughed. After that, we walked in single file, not from strategy, but because there was no room. I watched Noor’s backpack move in front of me, her shoulders rising and falling, and thought about how little control we actually have once we’re past the well-marked paths.

It wasn’t fear, exactly. It was the slow accumulation of wrong details—the absence of bird calls, the way shadows stretched too long even at mid-morning, the realization that none of the landmarks matched anything we remembered. We didn’t say much. Every time one of us opened our mouth, it was to correct a direction, propose a bearing, then retract it when another bent tree or silent patch of ground made it irrelevant.

Eventually, we stumbled onto something resembling a road—more a wide track of packed dirt and tire marks. It didn’t feel like rescue. It felt like interruption. We followed it down, shoulders stiff, not speaking. The car was where we left it, ordinary, sun-warmed on one side. We dumped our packs in silence, unscrewed water bottles with too much force.

I keep thinking about the rusted can. The way Petra turned it in her hand, trying to make out faded lettering. Like proof we’d been somewhere, even if we couldn’t name it. Like maybe being lost is just another kind of place. We’d passed through, and carried nothing back.

It started easy enough—dirt path, morning light still slanting low through the trees, that crisp September smell in the air that never lasts past ten a.m. I’d looked at a map the night before, just a quick scroll on my phone, and traced what seemed like a straightforward loop. Five miles, maybe six, nothing we couldn’t do before lunch.

Ruth, Mateo, and I had agreed on meeting at the trailhead just after eight. Ruth brought the binoculars, of course. Mateo the thermos of coffee, already half gone. I had the map printed—on actual paper—and tucked it into my pack like it meant something. We were all wearing old hiking boots, the kind cracked at the seams but still functional, and we laughed at how the car smelled like wet dog even though none of us owned one.

Somewhere after the second fork, the cairns led us off the main trail. They looked intentional—not just random stacks, but deliberate, like someone had been there not too long ago. We followed them because why not? They curved uphill, into a part of the woods I didn’t remember from the map. Thicker trees, more moss. A silence settled in, not the peaceful kind, but the kind where you start noticing how far from a cell signal you really are.

We didn’t panic. Not then. But the trail narrowed to a deer path, then disappeared altogether. I unfolded the map again, but the landmarks didn’t match—no boulder field, no creek crossing like it showed. Just trees, and more trees, and the odd blue jay yelling from above like it was laughing.

We backtracked, but backtracking in the woods is never really backtracking. You end up in places that look familiar but aren’t, like memories distorted by time. Beatriz—if she’d been there—would’ve known what to do. She grew up climbing hills with her grandfather, could read landforms like sentences. But it was just the three of us, and none of us had that instinct.

Eventually, we heard water. A real rushing sound. We pushed toward it, half-expecting a river, but it was just a narrow stream, barely knee-deep. Mateo crouched and drank straight from it. I didn’t stop him. We followed the stream downhill because that’s what you do—water leads somewhere, doesn’t it?

When the paved road appeared—narrow, unmarked, with tire tracks and no guardrails—we didn’t celebrate. We just walked, boots squelching, until we saw the yellow tape around the trailhead sign. Some ranger had blocked the west fork with a handwritten note: “Unsafe After Storm.”

We drove back in silence, radio off. The coffee in the thermos was cold. Outside the window, the trees passed in blurry greens and browns, endless and indifferent. Back at my place, I left the boots by the door, still muddy. I don’t think I’ll look at the map again, not anytime soon. There’s something about being lost that stays with you, not as fear, but as a quiet hum beneath everything you thought you knew.

Repotting the snake plant went about as expected, which is to say: slowly, with some swearing, and one snapped leaf. It had been in the same pot since Ruth brought it over two apartments ago, back when we still thought succulents were easy. Now the roots had worked their way through the drainage hole and into the saucer beneath, knitting themselves into the soft plastic like they were building a nest. I tried soaking the root ball, turning the pot in my lap while water pooled on the newspaper, thinking warmth might loosen things. It didn’t.  

I got the tarp out, the one with the mildew spot near the corner that I keep meaning to wash. Dragged the plant, still half-potted, across the kitchen floor, leaving a trail of dried soil. The new container was wider, ceramic, picked more for looking decent in the corner than for any real suitability. The tag said “for medium to large foliage”—marketing nonsense. Nothing ever fits right. I had the old pot, the new one, a pair of kitchen scissors I didn’t want to ruin, a rusty hand trowel from the building’s shed, and a bag of that pre-mixed potting soil that always seems damp no matter how long it’s been open.  

Tried tipping the plant sideways, gripping the base, pulling. Nothing. Tried tapping the sides. Then rapping it on the edge of the sink. A small cloud of dust rose. Then, finally, a crack. The pot gave—plastic, not clay—split cleanly down one side. That helped, but the roots were matted, circling like ropes. I cut a few with the scissors, felt bad, kept going. One leaf tore near the base when I was tugging a particularly stubborn knot free. I set the damaged piece aside. Maybe it’ll root in water. Probably not.  

Felt the weight of the plant in my hands—dirt clumped in folds of my sleeves, soil down the front of my pants. The roots were pale, dense, some already turning brown at the tips. I loosened what I could with my fingers, broke apart small clumps, tried not to imagine it hurting. Scooped a handful of soil into the new pot. Placed the plant. It sat too high. Added more soil around, pressed down with the heel of my palm. Left a gap at the top, like the internet said. Poured water slowly. It pooled, then soaked in unevenly. One side absorbed, the other shed water like it was still in shock.  

Cleaned up, mostly. Scooped the loose dirt into the compost bucket. Wiped the floor with a dishrag that immediately turned brown. Left the torn leaf on the windowsill, out of obligation. Washed my hands three times. Soil under the nails, grit between fingers, residue behind the knuckles.  

Later, Sanjay came by. Looked at the plant, glanced at me. “Looks better,” he said. Maybe it did. Or maybe it just looked different—freed, displaced, uncertain. I didn’t correct him. We stood there a minute, both of us watching the light hit the leaves at an angle, long and flat now, the way it does in the late afternoon.  

I unplugged the fan because it was making the stems shiver, and turned it toward the wall instead. Left the window open a crack, though there wasn’t much breeze.

Repotting the weeping fig again. It's the third time in two years, though it doesn’t look like much has changed. The root mass is still dense, circling the inner pot in tight loops—fibrous and light brown, some starting to press through the drainage holes from below. The old pot, terracotta, about 18 cm in diameter, showed signs of stress: fine cracking near the base, possibly from moisture retention and repeated drying cycles. I’ve learned over time not to force it, but today required more coaxing than usual. Inverted the plant, tapped the base—no movement. Applied upward pressure on the stem while rotating the pot slightly; still no release. Used a narrow trowel to work around the inner edge, loosening compacted soil, careful not to damage lower cortex tissue. Eventually, it slid out with a soft pop, roots matted and spiraling.

The new container is plastic, slightly larger—22 cm diameter, with a modest increase in depth. Drainage holes are sufficient, though I’ll need to monitor saturation. Mixed a fresh batch of potting medium: base of peat substitute (coir-based, moderately decomposed), roughly 60%, perlite for aeration (30%), and a small inclusion of pumice (10%) for weight and structure. No fertilizer added—last feed was six weeks ago, and leaching was visible on the outer rim of the old pot, a pale calcified residue. Mixed thoroughly in a shallow tray to avoid clumping, then dampened with room-temperature water until just moist. Over-wetting at this stage risks compaction and anaerobic conditions once settled.

Root pruning was minimal. Trimmed only the thickest outer loops—the ones that had begun to constrict inward—with clean bypass shears. About 1.5 cm removed from the circumference. No visible rot, but one section showed early signs of callousing, possibly from prior overwatering. Left intact. Fluffed gently with fingers to encourage outward growth once repotted.

Centered the plant in the new pot. Filled around the sides in stages, pressing lightly after each addition to eliminate major air gaps without compressing the medium. Watered in slowly with a fine rose attachment—approx. 400 mL distributed over three minutes. No runoff initially; absorption was slow, suggesting some degree of hydrophobicity in the old root ball. Second pass at ten-minute intervals until drainage began. Collected the leachate; volume was low, slightly cloudy, neutral pH when tested.

Placed in the usual spot—northwest-facing, indirect light, near the reading chair. Humidity is stable, between 45–50% today. Ambient temp 21°C. Leaves appear turgid, no signs of wilt, though two lower fronds were removed pre-repot due to yellowing and marginal necrosis. Probably nutrient imbalance from earlier overfertilization, not current stress. Thao said maybe the pot was too small, but that didn’t match the growth pattern. Ingrid suggested changing soil composition altogether, but I’m skeptical—she repotted her monstera six times last year, nothing took. Sanjay’s calathea is doing well, but different genus, different needs.

Checked again after two hours. Minor settling, but no drastic shift. Left the saucer unemptied for now—will assess in the morning. The process feels inconclusive. It’s impossible to know whether it’s enough, or even correct. The plant doesn’t communicate in real time. It will either grow or it won’t. There’s no middle state, no report. Just time, and observation.

The scissors are still on the counter.

Got the fiddle leaf fig out of the plastic pot today. It fought me the whole way. Roots were circling the bottom like it had nowhere else to go — tight and pale, like a nervous system wound too tight. Pried at them with my fingers, breaking a few, felt bad. Soil crumbled in patches, held together mostly by root, not earth. Should’ve done this months ago.  

Tried sliding it into the new pot — too big, maybe — but no, it didn’t fit. Kept tilting sideways, like it was leaning away from me. Had to kneel on the floor, steady it between my knees, scoop in the fresh mix with one hand. Dropped dirt everywhere. Bit of it got in my sock.  

Beatriz came by later, said it looked lopsided. I said maybe it’ll even out. She touched a leaf, said it felt dry. I hadn’t watered it fully yet — wanted to wait, let it settle. Now I wonder if that was dumb.  

Left it near the window, but the light’s different this time of year. Slants in low, hits the wall too soon. Pot’s heavy, terra cotta. Hard to move once it’s in place. I shifted it three times already.  

One leaf snapped when I was wiping dust off. Just came clean off. I left it on the desk. Might try to root it, though I doubt it will.  

Amara says these things go through phases, like people. Stops growing, then takes off. I don’t know. Seems like waiting.  

Floor’s still dusty. Got a clump stuck under the radiator. I’ll get it eventually. For now, the plant’s upright, at least. Leans a little to the left, but nothing a folded napkin beneath the pot can’t fix. Probably won’t fix it. Just leave it.  

Washed my hands, but my nails still look like I was digging in someone’s backyard. Maybe it’ll settle in. Maybe it won’t. Leaves are quiet tonight. No way to tell.
